note:
我们仍在等待测试惠普的下一款PC-VR头盔，售价600美元的惠普G2，但在11月份开始向预购客户发货之前，惠普已经公布了下一个VR促销活动。而且这是一个生物特征跟踪的小玩意儿。
HP G2 Omnicept Edition提供您在G2中能找到的一切，包括一对高分辨率、快速切换的LCD面板；一个由内而外的跟踪解决方案；镜头、扬声器以及借用自Valve Index的其他优化功能；以及HP更新版的Windows混合现实控制器。
但这款高端机型针对的是拥有丰富内置传感器的企业客户，它的发布窗口很模糊，2021年春季发布，目前还没有定价。这些设备包括：眼睛跟踪传感器和瞳孔测量传感器，分别确定你的凝视和瞬间的扩张；心率传感器；以及面部捕捉摄像头，将你的样子翻译给其他用户。(惠普还没有向我们展示面部捕捉系统的工作原理，他们已经确认，Omnicept发布的SDK中不会包含它的一些功能。)。
惠普小心翼翼地不将这些传感器中的任何一个附加到游戏或看电影等消费级娱乐选项上。相反，该公司的公关团队主要将这款耳机作为训练演习的有用解决方案-并毫不含糊地轻推了一下，在我们这个持续隔离的时代，高强度场景的虚拟化版本可能会被证明是有用的。(然而，如果没有一个明确的发布窗口，我们不知道在新冠肺炎疫苗生效之前，我们是否会看到G2 Omnicept。)。
在我还没来得及说出我的及时担忧之前，惠普就向我抛出了三个出于隐私考虑的要点，向我抛出了一张幻灯片。首先，HP Omnicept SDK为开发人员提供了捕获和转换生物特征数据所需的全套API，其核心是符合GDPR。其次，惠普向企业级Omnicept客户提供的云基础设施承诺对其处理的所有用户数据进行识别、聚合和保护。
最后，惠普表示，在捕获过程中，惠普耳机本身在遵守GDPR的法律框架内保护数据，耳机上没有存储任何数据。这仍然让个别公司将这些耳机及其兼容的培训应用程序交给员工和学生，并决定如何处理通过任何一组训练序列或测试收集到的丰富的个人信息，特别是情绪反应(由心率和瞳孔传感器追踪)。
隐私是[Omnicept]对话的开始。他的顾问、虚拟人互动实验室创始总监杰里米·拜伦森(Jeremy Bailenson)在谈到Omnicept将作为产品线推出时表示，该公司将公开科学家可以获取的有关G2 Omnicept传感器工作原理的数据-尽管具体会推出哪些数据，以及如何推出，仍有待观察。
惠普产品经理斯科特·罗林斯声称，其Omnicept SDK将为开发人员提供认知负荷API。例如，罗林斯描述了这样一个场景：训练员在操控困难的模拟(比如驾驶飞机穿越每小时60英里的风速)时，了解某人可能承受的压力有多大，或者通过观察工程师在练习和接受进一步指导后精神状态的改善，来追踪训练方案的效果。当然，没过多久，我就想到了雇主掌握有关员工认知负荷的微调数据可能带来的潜在危害。
眼球跟踪系统还有一个不那么令人毛骨悚然的好处，那就是它开启了惠普G2 Omnicept对凹陷渲染的支持。Omnicept的SDK可以跟随眼睛的方向，动态调整3D渲染，以模糊VR用户没有直视的任何像素，从而提高性能效率。惠普表示，其SDK的这一部分将向广大开发者开放，这对未来的耳机或添加到现有耳机上的第三方眼球跟踪系统来说可能是个好消息。
注意到上面所有关于培训和员工的评论了吗？那是故意的。惠普已经明确表示，这款头戴式耳机将不会瞄准消费级市场，这似乎更多地表明了其零售计划(阅读：减少与零售商的合作，更多地是与企业客户的个案销售基础)，以及可能的价格窗口(阅读：高于600美元的G2)。罗林斯谈到购买这款游戏头戴式耳机的潜力时说，除非(客户)有支持这一功能的软件，否则[Omnicept的特殊功能]不会为他们做太多事情(尽管它至少在默认情况下会支持SteamVR)。
note_en:
We're still waiting to test out HP's next PC-VR headset, the  $600 HP G2, but before it begins shipping to preorder customers in November, HP has already unveiled its next VR sales pitch. And it's a biometric-tracking doozy.
 The HP G2 Omnicept Edition delivers everything you'll find in the G2, including a pair of high-res, fast-switching LCD panels; an "inside-out" tracking solution; lenses, speakers, and other optimizations borrowed from Valve Index; and HP's updated version of the Windows Mixed Reality controllers.
 But this higher-tier version, which has a vague "Spring 2021" launch window and no price yet, is aimed squarely at enterprise customers with a wealth of built-in sensors. These include: eye-tracking  and pupillometry sensors, to separately determine your gaze and your moment-to-moment dilation; a heart rate sensor; and a facial-capture camera, to translate how you look to other users. (HP has not yet shown us how that facial-capture system will work, and they've confirmed that some of its features will not be part of the Omnicept's launch SDK.)
  HP was careful not to attach any of these sensors' potential to consumer-level entertainment options like games or movie-watching. Instead, the company's PR team talked primarily about this headset as a useful solution for training exercises—with a not-so-subtle nudge about how virtualized versions of intense scenarios may prove useful during our continued quarantine era. (Without a firm launch window, however, there's no telling whether we'll see the G2 Omnicept before  a working COVID-19 vaccine.)
 Before I could pipe up with  my timely concerns, HP shoved a slide in my face with three privacy-minded bullet points. First, the HP Omnicept SDK, which gives developers the full suite of APIs needed to capture and translate biometric data, has been developed with GDPR compliance at its core. Second, HP's cloud infrastructure offer to enterprise-grade Omnicept customers promises to "de-identify, aggregate, and secure" all user data that it processes.
 Last of all, HP says the headset itself "secures data during capture within a legal framework that adheres to GDPR, and no data is stored on the headset." That still leaves data handling up to individual firms who hand these headsets, and their compatible training apps, to staffers and students—and decide what to do with the wealth of personal information, particularly emotional responses (as tracked by heartrate and pupil sensors), gleaned by any battery of training sequences or tests.
 "Privacy was the beginning of the [Omnicept] conversations," consultant and Virtual Human Interaction Lab Founding Director Jeremy Bailenson said about the rollout of Omnicept as a product line, and he alleged that the company will "open up" data that scientists can access related to how G2 Omnicept's sensors work—though exactly what data will roll out and how remains to be seen.
  HP Product Manager Scott Rawlings claims that its Omnicept SDK will go so far as to give developers "APIs for cognitive load." As an example, Rawlings described a scenario where trainers understand how stressed someone might be when handling difficult simulations such as piloting a plane through 60MPH winds, or to track how effective a training regimen is by seeing an engineer's mental state improve after practicing and receiving further instruction. It didn't take me long to think of the potential evils that might come from employers having fine-tuned data about staffers' cognitive load, of course.
 One less-creepy bonus about the eye-tracking system is that it opens up HP G2 Omnicept's support for "foveated rendering." The Omnicept's SDK can "follow the direction of the eye" and adjust 3D rendering on the fly to blur whatever pixels a VR user is  not looking directly at, and thus make performance more efficient. HP says that this part of its SDK will be open to developers at large, which is possibly good news for future headsets or third-party eye-tracking systems added to existing headsets.
 Notice all of the comments above about training and employees? That's intentional. HP has made it clear that the headset will not be aimed at the consumer-grade market, which seems as much an indication of its retail plan (read: less collaboration with retailers, more of a case-by-case sales basis with enterprise customers) as its likely price window (read: higher than the $600 G2). "Unless [customers] have software that supports this functionality, [Omnicept's special features] are not going to do much for them," Rawlings says about the potential of buying this as a gaming headset (though it will support SteamVR by default, at least).
title: 惠普G2 Omnicept：传感器可以读取“认知负荷”的企业级VR
title_en: HP G2 Omnicept: Enterprise-grade VR whose sensors can read “cognitive load”

note:
VTEX为沃尔玛(Walmart)等零售商和百威英博(AB InBev)、摩托罗拉(Motorola)、斯坦利·布莱克(Stanley Black&amp；Decker)、索尼(Sony)、沃尔玛(Walmart)、惠而浦(Whirlpool)、可口可乐(Coca-Cola)和雀巢(Nestlé)等大型消费品品牌提供电子商务解决方案和战略。VTEX已经筹集了2.25亿美元的新资金，公司估值为17亿美元。
这笔融资由老虎全球(Tiger Global)和龙松资本(Lone Pine Capital)两家投资者共同牵头，星座(Constulation)、奋进催化剂(Endeavour Catalyst)和软银(SoftBank)也参与其中。这家初创公司的首席战略官兼北美总经理萨德·阿米特·沙阿(Sad Amit Shah)表示，这是一个投资者的组合，有两条线索，为这家初创公司的下一步可能会发生什么提供了一个“信号”。
“我们看到他们在公司上市前大举投资，”他说。“现在，这不一定会在这里发生，但这是一个信号。”沙阿说，该公司一直盈利，并计划继续盈利(这使其成为软银投资没有失败的一个例子)。今年的收入增长了114%，通过它建立的平台处理的商品交易总额(GMV)达到80亿美元。
鉴于VTEX上一次融资是在不到一年前-由软银(SoftBank)的拉丁美洲创新基金(拉丁美洲创新基金)牵头的一轮1.4亿美元-对这家初创公司的估值跃升是巨大的。Shah向我们证实，它的估值比之前的估值(4.25亿美元)增加了4倍。
去年11月，软银拉美基金的兴趣源于VTEX成立之初。该公司一开始为希望打入巴西和拉美其他地区的企业建造电子商务店面和战略。巴西是世界上最大的新兴“金砖四国”市场之一。他补充说，该公司在该地区建立沃尔玛成名，即使在沃尔玛剥离资产后，它也继续帮助运营和发展这项业务，目前它也在美国以外的其他地区与沃尔玛合作。
但自那以后，尽管该业务的拉美部门继续蓬勃发展，但该公司利用获得的资金，以及目前全球对电子商务解决方案的环境，将业务扩展到更多市场，特别是北美、欧洲、中东和非洲地区，最近还有亚洲。
沙阿说，在大流行之前，收入每年以50%的速度增长，而在今年最近的增长114%之前。“当然，我们不希望新冠肺炎在这里，但这对我们的业务产生了很好的影响。电子商务的发展对收入产生了影响，并增加了投资者的兴趣。“。
VTEX的成功不仅取决于迎合那些到目前为止还没有优先考虑其在线渠道的公司，而且还取决于以一种更统一的方式做到这一点。
由于消费者包装商品的增长方式支离破碎，它们一直处于多方面的束缚中。一个饮料品牌不仅会在当地生产(有时，比如可口可乐，使用不同的配料配方)，而且他们的产品通常只在特定的市场销售，而且因为受众不同，他们也在当地制定了营销和分销策略。最重要的是，像这样的产品长期以来一直依赖零售商、餐馆、自动售货机等渠道将产品送到消费者手中。
当然，如今所有这一切都被打乱了：他们原本用来销售商品的所有传统渠道现在要么关闭了，要么看到客户大幅减少。至于营销：社交网络的兴起导致了消息传递的全球化，一些东西可以像病毒一样在世界各地传播，因此营销没有地域界限。
因此，所有这些都意味着品牌必须重新考虑如何销售其产品，这就是像VTEX这样的公司介入的地方，建立了可以在多个地区使用的战略和解决方案。在典型的交易中，它一直在与百威英博(AB InBev)合作开发一个覆盖50个国家的全球商务平台(取代来自其他供应商的多种产品，通常VTEX的竞争对手包括SAP、Shopify和Magento)。
沙阿说：“中央人民政府的公司正在寻求标准化，让他们的业务和生活变得更容易一些。”它所做的典型工作包括为零售商建立市场，或建立新的电子商务接口，以便品牌可以更好地供给线上和线下零售商，或直接向客户销售-例如，使用新的订购产品的方式由他人送货。Shah说，VTEX现在已经为其客户建立了大约200个市场。
(值得一提的是，沙阿本人有创业和电子商务的血统。他创立了一家名为Jirafe的电子商务分析公司，该公司被SAP收购，随后他成为SAP Hybra的首席营收官。)。
VTEX联合创始人兼联席首席执行官马里亚诺·戈米德·德·法里亚(Mariano Gomide De Faria)在一份声明中表示：“我们很高兴能在新的和现有的市场上快速增长，并为更多品牌提供一个拥抱商业未来的平台，这就是协作，利用市场，提供首屈一指的客户体验。”“这笔资金的注入无疑将支持我们实现在全球加速数字商业转型的使命.”
note_en:
VTEX, which builds e-commerce solutions and strategies for retailers like Walmart and huge consumer names like AB InBev, Motorola, Stanley Black & Decker, Sony, Walmart, Whirlpool, Coca-Cola and Nestlé, has raised $225 million in new funding, valuing the company at $1.7 billion post-money.
 The funding is being co-led by two investors, Tiger Global and Lone Pine Capital, with Constellation, Endeavour Catalyst and SoftBank also participating. It’s a mix of investors, with two leads, that offers a “signal” of what might come next for the startup, sad Amit Shah, the company’s chief strategy officer and general manager for North America.
  “We’ve seen them invest in big rounds right before companies go public,” he said. “Now, that’s not necessarily happening here right now, but it’s a signal.” The company has been profitable and plans to continue to be, Shah said (making it one example of a SoftBank investment that hasn’t gone sour). Revenues this year are up 114% with $8 billion in gross merchandise volume (GMV) processed over platforms it’s built.
 Given that VTEX last raised money less than a year ago — a  $140 million round led by SoftBank’s Latin American Innovation Fund — the valuation jump for the startup is huge. Shah confirmed to us that it represents a 4x increase on its previous valuation (which would have been $425 million).
 The interest back in November from SoftBank’s Latin American fund stemmed from VTEX’s beginnings. The company got its start building e-commerce storefronts and strategies for businesses that were hoping to break into Brazil — the B of the world’s biggest emerging “BRIC” markets — and the rest of Latin America. It made its name building Walmart in the region, and has continued to help run and develop that operation even after Walmart divested the asset, and it’s working with Walmart now in other regions outside the US, too, he added.
 But since then, while the Latin American arm of the business has continued to thrive, the company has capitalized both on the funding it had picked up, and the current global climate for e-commerce solutions, to expand its business into more markets, specifically North America, EMEA and most recently Asia.
 Revenues were growing at a rate of 50% a year before the pandemic ahead of it’s more recent growth this year of 114%, Shah said. “Of course, we would prefer Covid-19 not to be here, but it has had a good effect on our business. The arc of e-commerce has grown has impacted revenues and created that additional level of investor interest.”
 VTEX’s success has hinged not just on catering to companies that have up to now not prioritized their online channels, but in doing so in a way that is more unified.
 Consumer packaged goods have been in a multi-faceted bind because of the fragmented way in which they have grown. A drinks brand will not only manufacture on a local level (and sometimes, as in the case of, say, Coca-Cola, use different ingredient formulations), but they will often have products that are only sold in select markets, and because the audiences are different, they’ve devise marketing and distribution strategies on a local level, too. On top of all that, products like these have long relied on channels like retailers, restaurants, vending machines and more to get their products into the hands of consumers.
 These days, of course, all of that has been disrupted: all the traditional channels they would have used to sell things are now either closed or seeing greatly reduced custom. And as for marketing: the rise of social networks has led to a globalization in messaging, where something can go viral all over the world and marketing therefore knows no regional boundaries.
 So, all of this means that brands have to rethink everything around how they sell their products, and that’s where a company like VTEX steps in, building strategies and solutions that can be used in multiple regions. Among typical deals, it’s been working with AB InBev to develop a global commerce platform covering 50 countries (replacing multiple products from other vendors, typically competitors to VTEX include SAP, Shopify and Magento).
 “CPG companies are seeking to standardize and make their businesses and lives a little easier,” Shah said. Typical work that it does includes building marketplaces for retailers, or new e-commerce interfaces so that brands can better supply online and offline retailers, or sell directly to customers — for example, with new ways of ordering products to get delivered by others. Shah said that some 200 marketplaces have now been built by VTEX for its customers.
 (Shah himself, it’s worth pointing out, has a pedigree in startups and in e-commerce. He founded an e-commerce analytics company called Jirafe, which was acquired by SAP, where he then became the chief revenue officer of SAP Hybris.)
 “We are excited to grow quickly in new and existing markets, and offer even more brands a platform that embraces the future of commerce, which is about being collaborative, leveraging marketplaces, and delivering customer experiences that are second-to-none,” said Mariano Gomide de Faria, VTEX co-founder and co-CEO, in a statement. “This injection of funding will undoubtedly support us in achieving our mission to accelerate digital commerce transformation around the world.”
title: VTEX以17亿美元的估值筹集2.25亿美元，用于针对零售商和品牌的电子商务解决方案
title_en: VTEX raises $225M at a $1.7B valuation for e-commerce solutions aimed at retailers and brands

note:
Sethi最近担任云数据管理Rubrik的首席信息安全官，之前曾在IBM、Palo Alto Networks和Intuit担任网络安全职务。
在Twitter监督公司信息安全实践和政策的新角色中，Sethi将向平台负责人Nick Tornow汇报工作，根据她宣布这一工作调动的推文。
很高兴能成为团队的一员@Twitter与@icktornow@michaelmontano@tala一起工作，还有更多！Https://t.co/SGoBLr5957。
Sethi还担任几家初创公司的顾问，包括LevelOps和Authomize，以及网络安全组织，包括女性参与网络安全。
自前安全主管迈克·科夫蒂诺(Mike Convertino)于去年12月离职加入网络弹性公司Arceo以来，Twitter一直空缺着首席信息安全官的职位。
今年7月，该公司受到了一场针对该公司内部“管理”工具的非常公开的网络攻击，攻击在社交媒体平台上实时上演，黑客劫持了备受瞩目的Twitter账户，以传播一场加密货币骗局。黑客使用了语音钓鱼，这是一种社会工程技术，涉及在电话上欺骗某人交出密码或访问内部系统。
本月早些时候，该公司表示，在攻击发生后，它加强了安全，包括推出安全密钥，这使得针对Twitter的那种攻击变得更加困难。
note_en:
Sethi most recently served as chief information security officer at cloud data management Rubrik, and previously worked in cybersecurity roles at IBM, Palo Alto Networks, and Intuit.
 In the new role at  Twitter   overseeing the company’s information security practices and policies, Sethi will report to platform lead, Nick Tornow, according to  her tweet announcing the job move.
 So excited to be a part of the team  @twitter working alongside  @nicktornow  @michaelmontano  @paraga and more!  https://t.co/SGoBLr5957
 Sethi also serves as an advisor to several startups, including LevelOps and Authomize, and cybersecurity organizations, including  Women in Cybersecurity.
 Twitter had left the role of chief information security officer vacant since the departure of its previous security chief, Mike Convertino, who left in December to join cyber resilience firm Arceo.
 In July, the company was hit by a very public cyberattack on  the company’s internal “admin” tools that played out on the social media platform in real time, as hackers hijacked high profile Twitter accounts to  spread a cryptocurrency scam. The hackers used voice phishing, a social engineering technique that involves tricking someone on the phone to hand over passwords or access to internal systems.
 Earlier this month, the company  said it bolstered its security following the attack, including rolling out security keys, which makes the kind of attack that targeted Twitter far more difficult.
title: 入侵事件发生后，Twitter聘请了一位新的网络安全主管
title_en: After breach, Twitter hires a new cybersecurity chief

note:
斯坦·楚德诺夫斯基不记得首席执行官马克·扎克伯格和其他Facebook高管何时开始谈论将公司的服务组合更像是一个投资组合。这位现任Facebook Messenger服务副总裁只是回忆说，随着Facebook、Messenger、Instagram和WhatsApp的用户数量远远超过10亿大关，公司最初将Facebook、Messenger、Instagram和WhatsApp几乎分开的倾向开始变得越来越笨拙。Chudnovsky说，高管们没有继续孤立每一款应用程序，而是得出结论说，“如果我们正在打造的体验更具互联性，我们就能更好地为人们服务。”“这个事实已经在我们耳边回响了很长一段时间。但从来不明显的是，我们将如何进行这项工作，以及我们将如何优先处理这项工作。“。
为了启动这一过程，扎克伯格做了他经常做的事情：他在Facebook上发布了这一消息。2019年3月6日，他分享了一份3219字的宣言，题为“以隐私为中心的社交网络愿景”。他将社交网络的“城市广场”(Facebook和Instagram Feed提供的主要是公共会议区域)与更私密的“起居室”进行了对比，比如通过Messenger或WhatsApp进行的私人群聊和信息交流。
扎克伯格写道：“今天，我们已经看到，私信、昙花一现的故事和小团体是目前增长最快的在线交流领域。”他详细介绍了一项雄心勃勃的、长达数年的计划，要将Facebook的服务组合朝着这个方向积极推进--而且全部一起推进。Facebook、Instagram、WhatsApp和Messenger将遵循统一的战略实现这一目标。
那是近19个月前的事了。Facebook随后在很大程度上对这一努力保持沉默，这一努力的规模和复杂性使得即使是项目中较简单的部分也变成了充满潜在陷阱的巨大项目。但该公司终于开始推出扎克伯格新愿景的关键组成部分。
9月29日，Facebook推出了账户中心(Accounts Center)，这是一个仍处于测试模式的一站式中心，用户可以在这里查看和更改Facebook、Messenger和Instagram的设置，包括登录每项服务并在它们之间自动交叉发布的能力。现在，它也开始推出新版本的Instagram即时通讯功能，该功能基于Messenger，而不是它自己的独立实体。这两款服务的用户都可以通过这两款应用程序与Messenger和Instagram好友聊天。
由于整合计划中一些最棘手的方面尚未实现，例如跨越Facebook所有消息产品的端到端加密，本周的声明只是一个开始。但它们仍然代表着一个有意义的支点。长期担任Facebook产品沙皇的克里斯·考克斯(Chris Cox)表示：“公司的第一个十年实际上专注于更多的公共空间--馈送、故事、大型团体。”扎克伯格发表这篇帖子后不久，他就离开了公司，然后于去年6月重新加盟，担任首席产品官。随着本周公布的功能公布，“如果我们成功了，从长远来看，我们距离实现目标还有几个百分点，因为我们还有很多建设要做。”
使用Facebook服务组合的24.7亿人将如何应对这些变化--还有更多变化--即使对Facebook来说也是未知的。虽然扎克伯格(该公司没有让他参与这篇报道)经常对Facebook的未来发表声明，但现实生活并不总是合作的。例如，在2014年，他大胆预测，用户生成的视频将在五年内主导该网站。现在已经过去六年了，我们的新闻馈送仍然充斥着文本。然而，很容易理解为什么他会把目光投向他主持的巨大(仍然利润丰厚的)城镇广场之外。这不仅仅是因为越来越多的私人应用程序越来越受欢迎。
尽管私信有其自身的问题--What­App和Messenger都对消息转发设置了限制，以减缓错误信息的传播--但困扰Facebook的争议很大程度上源于它的公开性。网站上分享的任何虚假信息、恶作剧和仇恨言论都可以在瞬间传播给数百万人，这一事实使该公司成为政客和监管机构的目标。就连Facebook的一些员工--历来都是他们工作公司的忠实信仰者--也进行了反击，他们在推特上表达了自己的不满，并在6月份举行了一场虚拟的罢工。
扎克伯格发表这篇帖子后，随着2020年现实世界的残酷动荡-包括新冠肺炎疫情、警察击毙乔治·弗洛伊德引发的抗议活动以及对美国总统选举诚信的威胁-涌入脸书，然后又回到现实世界，扎克伯格的聚光灯只会变得更加炙手可热。该公司对混乱局面的处理招致了自己的反对，特别是它拒绝压制唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)总统在一篇帖子中警告称，“当抢劫开始时，枪击就开始了。”由于担心与Facebook的关联，1200个品牌暂停了在Facebook上的广告-至少在7月份暂停了广告-这是一项名为“为利润而停止仇恨”的举措的一部分。该公司的收入受到的打击可以忽略不计。
对Facebook权力的担忧并不是什么新鲜事，但要求对其采取行动的呼声越来越高，而且愈演愈烈。在扎克伯格透露他对公司的新愿景两个月后，他的哈佛同学、Facebook的联合创始人克里斯·休斯在“纽约时报”上发表了一篇煽动性的评论文章，题为“是时候拆分Facebook了”。去年7月，众议员大卫·西西林(D-RI)在美国众议院司法反垄断小组委员会听证会上质问扎克伯格说：“你们的平台太大了，即使有正确的政策，你们也不可能包含致命的内容。坦率地说，我相信它击中了美国民主的核心。“。
“监视资本主义时代”的作者Shoshana Zuboff说，“社会中草根阶层的动员越来越多，我们曾经认为是解决问题的[科技巨头]现在成了问题的症结所在。在我看来，正是这种公众不安和公众恐惧感的转变，最终动员了立法者。“。
没有办法将这种观点与Facebook的自我形象相提并论。产品和社会公益副总裁娜奥米·格莱特在斯坦福大学的论文中提到了Facebook，并于2005年加入该公司，当时该公司成立不到一年半。她至今仍在感叹，“人们认为我们的意图与我们的意图之间存在差距。”但事实仍然是，Facebook正在将其应用程序合并为一个更大、更强大的网络，同时许多人质疑它是否应该以目前的形式存在。
事实上，Facebook的大规模重组努力同时伴随着几个外部威胁：它的规模，它的主要收入来源(定向广告)，以及它对平台上发布的内容的责任保护。所有这些都使得这对公司来说是一个前所未有的时刻，而且--因为这就是我们谈论的Facebook--社会本身。
曾经有一段时间，Facebook对其组成部分的战略是更多地将它们视为自己的实体。Instagram联合创始人、时任首席执行官凯文·希斯特罗姆(Kevin Systrom)在2017年告诉我：“我喜欢人们来到Instagram校园，因为并不是每个人都意识到我们实际上是多么自主。”那是在他的公司从Facebook位于加利福尼亚州门洛帕克(Menlo Park)的庞大住宅区(感觉像是世界上最大的宿舍)搬到一英里半外自己闪闪发光的总部之后不久--不可避免地，总部里装饰着放大的照片，描绘了Instagram用户最美好的生活。
当时，希斯特罗姆将他与扎克伯格和Facebook首席运营官谢丽尔·桑德伯格的关系描述为首席执行官和值得信任的董事会成员之间的关系。“你得到投入，你得到指导，”他解释说。“但你也获得了纯粹的自主权，你可以推动你的愿景向前发展-这也恰好也推动了大公司的愿景向前发展。”
让Instagram成为Instagram是一笔不错的生意。当Facebook在2012年斥资10亿美元收购这家照片分享新秀时，它在短短18个月内就积累了令人印象深刻的3000万用户。几年后，Instagram在Facebook抵御Snapchat的战略中发挥了决定性作用，Snapchat曾两次拒绝扎克伯格的收购提议。随着Snapchat有趣而短暂的“故事”功能开始流行，Systrom的团队在2016年巧妙地利用了它，创造了一个广受欢迎的克隆，感觉它仍然属于Instagram。
Facebook是否曾向Instagram施压，要求其做出让人感觉不像Instagrammy的改变？绝对一点儿没错。但Systrom能够抵御与Facebook的交叉促销等入侵，这可能会破坏该应用程序的高雅美学。“我会看到这样的东西，然后它们就会消失，”一名前员工回忆道。“我们赢了那些比赛。”
如果说有什么不同的话，那就是WhatsApp本身仍然是一个公国。2014年，在Facebook 190亿美元的收购报价宣布后，但在清除最后的监管障碍之前，首席执行官简·库姆告诉我，一旦交易完成，他的公司“没有计划改变我们的执行方式”。但事实并非如此。
在初创公司的日子里，库姆和合伙人布莱恩·阿克顿(Brian Acton)一直强烈反对收集用户不必要的数据，并在他们的应用程序上投放大量广告。他们在2012年的一篇博客文章中警告说：“记住，当广告涉及到时，你，用户就是产品。”他们不需要提到Facebook的名字来表达他们的观点。收购后，随着WhatsApp在2016年初增长到10亿用户，它继续拒绝广告，捍卫隐私。同年4月，它开启了端到端加密，即使是Whats­App也无法在用户之间传输消息时对其进行解密。
Instagram和WhatsApp的创始人在Facebook内享有的自由令人惊叹，因为它的生命力很强。但正如今年史蒂文·利维(Steven Levy)的“脸书：内幕”(Facebook：The Inside Story)和莎拉·弗里尔(Sarah Frier)的“无过滤器：Instagram的内幕”(No Filter：The Inside Story of Instagram)。希斯特罗姆和Instagram联合创始人迈克·克里格(Mike Krieger)开始觉得扎克伯格故意让他们缺乏资源。库姆和阿克顿发现自己受到桑德伯格的压力，要求他们加入他们鄙视的定向广告。阿克顿于2017年9月辞职，后来他在推特上发表了挑衅性的言论：是时候了。#delete facebook。“。次年4月，库姆辞职了。希斯特罗姆和克里格五个月后离开了。
在创始人撤离后，Facebook的资深高管亚当·莫塞里(Adam Mosseri)和威尔·卡斯卡特(Will Cathcart)分别接管了Instagram和WhatsApp。两人都是扎克伯格信任的副手，之前曾负责Facebook的News Feed-为即将到来的统一计划做好准备。
多年来，调查显示，大多数人不知道Facebook拥有Instagram和Whats-App。这似乎常常对公司有利。逃离Facebook的人-认为它不够酷的青少年或担心隐私的老年用户-最终往往会花更多的时间在Facebook Inc.拥有的其他东西上。
然而，这种无知在2019年8月变得更加难以维持，当时Instagram和WhatsApp的启动屏幕开始宣布这些应用程序是“来自Facebook的”。战略和设计咨询公司Redscout的首席执行官伊万·凯瑟(Ivan Kayser)表示，将心爱的产品与四面楚歌的Facebook品牌捆绑在一起，是一种大胆的透明行为，他称Facebook愿意这样做是“非常积极的”。
Instagram产品副总裁维沙尔·沙阿(Vishal Shah)表示，对Facebook和其旗下其他服务之间的关系感到困惑是“长期的，对我们来说不是一件好事”。他指出，该公司的应用程序一直受益于某些逻辑整合和整合。例如，Instagram用户只需点击一下就可以将照片交叉发布到Facebook上。企业也有统一的工具，比如一个仪表盘，用于在Facebook和Instagram上与客户进行沟通。
但在许多情况下，Facebook愿意加倍努力，如果不是三倍或四倍的话。在Instagram故事成为热门话题后，2017年，Facebook、Messenger和Whats­App都推出了自己的想法--非常相似，但并不完全相同。这是一种构建新功能的低效方式。“现在，每次我们在Facebook Stories上创新，Instagram都要花三个月的时间在Instagram Stories中实现同样的功能，反之亦然，”Fidji Simo感叹道，他是Facebook应用程序的副总裁，负责该公司的旗舰产品。
这与去年春天推出的房间形成了鲜明对比，在新冠肺炎疫情来袭时，该公司提前几个月推出了一项Zoom风格的视频会议服务。虽然房间正式是Messenger的一部分，但也可以从Facebook、Instagram和WhatsApp启动。最近推出的其他产品，如Facebook Pay和Facebook商店，也遵循类似的“无处不在的一项服务”战略。(今年8月，该公司将所有与支付相关的举措合并为一个名为Facebook Financial的新集团，由前Messenger首席执行官大卫·马库斯领导，马库斯曾担任贝宝(PayPal)总裁。)。
新的功能，比如账户中心将Facebook和Instagram的设置聚合在一个地方，听起来可能很平凡。但考虑到像Instagram这样的应用程序的历史独立性和文化怪癖，即使是看似直截了当的调整也面临着一系列挑战。
例如，在Facebook上，人们被限制-至少在理论上-只能使用一个真实的账户。在Instagram上，化名是可以的，一些年轻人会有一个“假Instagram”或“finsta”：一个仅供亲密朋友使用的第二个秘密账户。任何人都不会希望他们的finsta帖子出现在Facebook上。Facebook产品总监、Instagram前增长主管乔治·李(George Lee)表示：“用户需要决定(Facebook和Instagram账户)可以相互关联，而且从我们的角度来看，这种控制应该站在他们这边。”
网站的编织在一起需要每个人都进行调整。Shah于2015年加入Instagram，当时Instagram还是一家更自给自足的公司。如今，他说，“我告诉人们，‘如果你因为怀念过去我们在真空中构建和实验的日子而来到这里，也许现在这里不适合你。’ ”(这并没有阻止Instagram最近发布了Reels，这是一个只能在Instagram内部使用的TikTok二重身；脸书表示目前没有将其推广到其他应用程序的计划。)。
扎克伯格计划中最棘手的部分是强化对私信的强调。在没有设定截止日期的情况下，他在2019年的帖子中表示，Messenger、Whats­App和Instagram的即时通讯功能-每一个功能在历史上都是一个有围墙的花园-的用户将能够在公司内部称为“互操作”的项目中，通过这三项服务进行安全的交流。第一个切实的成果是新版本的Messenger。它仍然是一个独立的应用程序-但同样的功能出现在Instagram中，以取代其现有的消息功能，自2013年首次亮相以来，Instagram Direct一直被称为Instagram Direct。这一更新目前正在几个国家上线，并在全球范围内推出(包括在美国)。计划在2020年晚些时候举行。
在一定程度上，将Instagram的即时通讯功能转变为Messenger的应用内版本是为了提高效率。首席产品官考克斯说，这将允许公司“做研究，找出最好的产品，制造最好的产品，然后把它带来。”Instagram用户可以选择是切换还是保留现有形式的Instagram Direct；有很多诱使他们采取行动，包括新的自拍贴纸、动画文字效果、自定义表情符号反应、类似Snapchat的消失消息，以及共享视频观看功能-以及在Messenger上给朋友发消息的功能。
Facebook表示，它没有计划强迫每一位Instagram用户接受这一新体验。然而，该公司似乎不太可能在这款应用程序中无限期地保持两种风格的即时通讯功能。那些将Instagram视为躲避Facebook的避难所的人，可能会将Messenger衍生的功能视为一种侵犯，而不是升级。但考克斯说，考试给他带来了鼓舞：“它不会打断任何习惯、模式或人们真正喜欢的东西。我们对到目前为止在使用它的人们身上看到的情况感到非常满意。“
新的Instagram消息功能还包括一些迹象，表明Facebook考虑到了推倒服务之间的围墙对隐私的影响。例如，Instagram用户可以关闭从Messenger接收消息的功能。或者，他们可以自动将特定类型的传入邮件-比如来自Facebook朋友的朋友的邮件-路由到邮件请求，在那里它们被隔离而不是显示。甚至有一个功能可以根据你在Facebook上屏蔽的人来建议你可能想要屏蔽的账户。
“Facebook的每个人现在都明白，如果我们(隐私和安全)做得不好，我们就不会为Facebook的使命做出贡献，”目前从事跨应用体验工作的产品经理米图·辛格(Mitu Singh)说。“这在每个人的脑海里都是根深蒂固的。我认为除此之外，他们还意识到，如果我们做得不好，他们将无法发货他们想要发货的产品体验，他们对有人使用他们感到兴奋。“。
目前，WhatsApp还不是这一组合的一部分，考虑到这款应用给人的感觉更简约，任何将其与Messenger统一的举动都可能会让用户特别不安。但扎克伯格向Messenger和Instagram承诺的端到端加密技术也还在后面。Messenger副总裁Chudnovsky不愿透露互操作项目何时完成，只表示不会在今年完成。“我以为事情会很复杂，”他说。“我认为这比我们最初想象的要复杂一些.”
挑战不仅仅是技术上的。Facebook的历史上充斥着它为其庞然大物的广告业务服务而玩弄用户数据的例子。扎克伯格对更私密未来的愿景面临广泛的嘲讽的可能性是如此之高，以至于就连他也不得不承认这一点。他写道：“我理解，许多人认为Facebook不能或甚至不想建立这种以隐私为重点的平台--因为坦率地说，我们目前在构建隐私保护服务方面并没有很好的声誉，而且我们历史上一直专注于更开放的分享工具。”
至少扎克伯格可以指出Whats­App是一个典范。在心怀不满的创始人辞职很久之后，该服务的端到端加密和隐私的普遍声誉仍然完好无损。技术专家兼作家布鲁斯·施奈尔说：“他们没有把事情搞砸，这算是不错的。”不过他很快补充说，Facebook“不会做太多不让他们监视你的事情，因为他们的商业模式依赖于此。”
如果Facebook兑现承诺，将WhatsApp级别的隐私带给其他服务，围绕科技行业与法律关系的长期紧张局势可能会沸腾起来。政府讨厌端到端加密，因为它使信息不受标准监控技术的影响，即使在科技公司的帮助下也是如此。去年10月，美国司法部长威廉·巴尔(William Barr)以及英国和澳大利亚的官员签署了一封致扎克伯格的公开信，反对他在Facebook服务中推广端到端加密的计划，称这是对“儿童性剥削和虐待、恐怖主义以及外国对手试图破坏民主价值观和机构”的调查的灾难性阻碍。
执法人员经常建议在加密方案中增加一个“后门”，允许他们在适当的情况下访问数据。
.
note_en:
Stan Chudnovsky doesn’t remember when CEO Mark Zuckerberg and other top Facebook executives began talking about treating the company’s portfolio of services more like, well, a portfolio. The current VP for Facebook’s Messenger service simply recalls that the company’s original tendency to keep Facebook, Messenger, Instagram, and WhatsApp pretty much separate started to feel increasingly unwieldy as user numbers for each surged well past the billion mark. Instead of continuing to silo off each app, execs concluded that “we can serve people better if the experiences we are building are a little bit more interconnected,” Chudnovsky says. “That truth [had] been ringing in our ears for a long time. What was never obvious is how would we exactly go about it, and how would we prioritize it.”
   To kick-start the process, Zuckerberg did what he often does: He posted about it on Facebook. On March 6, 2019, he shared a  3,219-word manifesto titled “A Privacy-Focused Vision for Social Networking.” He drew a contrast between social networking’s “town squares”—the largely public meeting areas offered by Facebook and Instagram feeds—and its more intimate “living rooms,” as exemplified by private group chats and messages exchanged through Messenger or WhatsApp.
 “Today we already see that private messaging, ephemeral stories, and small groups are by far the fastest growing areas of online communication,” Zuckerberg wrote. He detailed a wildly ambitious, years-long plan to move Facebook’s portfolio of services aggressively in that direction—and all together. Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger would follow a unified strategy to get there.
 That was almost 19 months ago. Facebook then went largely quiet about the effort, which had a scale and complexity that made even the simpler parts of the undertaking into enormous projects fraught with potential pitfalls. But the company is finally beginning to roll out key components of Zuckerberg’s new vision.
 On September 29, Facebook  introduced Accounts Center, a one-stop hub—still in test mode—where users can view and change settings across Facebook, Messenger, and Instagram, including the ability to log into each service and automatically cross-post among them. Now it’s also beginning to push out  a new version of Instagram’s messaging features that’s based on Messenger rather being its own self-contained entity. Users on both services will be able to chat with Messenger and Instagram friends from either app.
 With some of the most thorny aspects of the integration plan not yet realized—such as end-to-end encryption spanning all of Facebook’s messaging products—this week’s announcements are just a start. But they still represent a meaningful pivot. “The first decade of the company was really focused on the more public space—feed, stories, large groups,” says Chris Cox, the longtime Facebook product czar who left the company shortly after Zuckerberg published his post, then rejoined as chief product officer last June. With the features being revealed this week, “if we’re successful, we’re a few [percentage points] of the way there in the long run, because we have so much more building to do.”
 How the 2.47 billion people who use Facebook’s portfolio of services will react to these changes—and more to come—is unknown even to Facebook. While Zuckerberg, whom the company did not make available for this story,  often makes proclamations about Facebook’s future, real life doesn’t always cooperate. In 2014, for example, he boldly predicted that user-generated video would dominate the site within five years. It’s now been six years, and our News Feeds are still laden with text. However, it’s easy to understand why he would be looking beyond the vast (and still vastly profitable) town squares he presides over. And it’s not just because the more private apps are gaining in popularity.
  Though private messaging has problems of its own—Whats­App and Messenger have both  placed limits on message forwarding to slow the spread of misinformation—much of the controversy that dogs Facebook stems from its publicness. The fact that any disinformation, hoaxes, and hateful rhetoric shared on the site can be disseminated to millions of people in an instant has made the company a target for politicians and watchdogs. Even some Facebook employees—­historically, true believers in the company they work for—have pushed back, tweeting their discontent and staging a virtual walkout in June.
 The spotlight has only grown hotter for Zuckerberg since he published his post, as 2020’s grinding real-world turmoil—including the COVID-19 pandemic, protests sparked by the police killing of George Floyd, and threats to the U.S. presidential election’s integrity—flowed onto Facebook and then back into the real world. The company’s handling of the chaos drew its own opposition, especially its refusal to suppress a post in which President Donald Trump warned that “when the looting starts, the shooting starts.” Nervous about their association with the social network, 1,200 brands paused their Facebook advertising—­­at least for the month of July—as part of a move called Stop Hate for Profit. The company’s revenues took a negligible hit.
  Concern about Facebook’s power isn’t new, but the push to do something about it has grown and intensified. Two months after Zuckerberg disclosed his new vision for the company, his Harvard classmate and Facebook cofounder Chris Hughes published an incendiary op-ed in  The New York Times, titled “It’s Time to Break Up Facebook.” This past July, Rep. David Cicilline (D-RI) grilled Zuckerberg at the U.S. House Judiciary Antitrust Subcommittee hearings, saying, “Your platform is so big, even with the right policies in place, you can’t contain deadly content. Frankly, I believe it strikes at the very heart of American democracy.”
 Shoshana Zuboff, author of  The Age of Surveillance Capitalism, says that “there is increasing mobilization within the society at the grassroots, the sense that [tech giants], which we once thought of as the solution, have now become the problem. In my view, it’s this shifting sense of public malaise, of public fear, that has finally mobilized lawmakers.”
 There’s no way to square this kind of take with Facebook’s self-image. VP of product and social good Naomi Gleit, who wrote about Facebook in her Stanford thesis and then joined the company in 2005 when it was less than a year and half old, still laments “the gap between what people think that our intentions are and what our intentions are.” But the fact remains that Facebook is combining its apps into one even bigger, formidable network at the same time that many are questioning whether it should exist at all in its current form.
 Indeed, Facebook’s massive reengineering effort coincides with several external threats at once: to its size, its primary revenue source (targeted advertising), and its liability protections for what’s published on the platform. All of which makes this an unprecedented moment for the company, and—since this is Facebook we’re talking about—society itself.
  There was a time when Facebook’s strategy for its component parts was to treat them more like entities unto themselves. “I love when people come to the Instagram campus, because not everyone realizes how autonomous we actually are,” Instagram cofounder and then CEO Kevin Systrom  told me in 2017. That was soon after his company had decamped from Facebook’s sprawling Menlo Park, California, digs, which felt like the world’s largest dorm room, to its own gleaming headquarters a mile and a half away—decorated, inevitably, with blown-up photos depicting Instagram users’ best lives.
 At the time, Systrom described his relationship with Zuckerberg and Facebook COO Sheryl Sandberg as being like that between a CEO and trusted board members. “You get input and you get guidance,” he explained. “But you also get pure autonomy, and you get to push your vision forward—which also happens to push the larger company’s vision forward as well.”
 Letting Instagram be Instagram was good business. When Facebook bought the photo-sharing phenom for $1 billion, in 2012, it had racked up an impressive 30 million users in just 18 months. Years later, Instagram played a decisive role in Facebook’s strategy for staving off Snapchat, which had twice spurned Zuckerberg’s acquisition proposals. As Snapchat’s playful, ephemeral “Stories” feature took off, Systrom’s team adroitly co-opted it in 2016, creating a wildly popular clone that still felt like it belonged inside Instagram.
 Did Facebook ever pressure Instagram to make changes that felt un-Instagrammy? Absolutely. But Systrom was able to fend off such intrusions as cross-promotions with Facebook that would have bespoiled the app’s tasteful aesthetic. “I’d see things like that, and then they’d just disappear,” remembers one former staffer. “We won those fights.”
 If anything, WhatsApp remained even more of a principality unto itself. In 2014, after Facebook’s $19 billion acquisition bid had been announced but before it had cleared its final regulatory hurdles, CEO Jan Koum  told me that his company had “no plans to change anything about how we execute” once the deal was done. It didn’t.
 In startup days, Koum and partner Brian Acton had been ardently opposed to collecting unnecessary data about users and larding their app with ads. “Remember, when advertising is involved,  you the user are the product,” they warned in  a 2012 blog post, not needing to mention Facebook by name to make their point. Post-acquisition, as WhatsApp grew to a billion users by early 2016, it continued to spurn ads and champion privacy. In April of that year, it switched on end-to-end encryption that prevented even Whats­App from deciphering messages as they traveled between users.
  The freedom that Instagram’s and WhatsApp’s founders enjoyed within Facebook was remarkable for its longevity. But as recounted this year in Steven Levy’s  Facebook: The Inside Story and Sarah Frier’s  No Filter: The Inside Story of Instagram, it wouldn’t last forever. Systrom and Instagram cofounder Mike Krieger came to feel that Zuckerberg was willfully starving them of resources. Koum and Acton found themselves under pressure from Sandberg to incorporate the targeted ads they despised. Acton  resigned in September 2017, later  tweeting a defiant “It is time. #deletefacebook.” Koum  quit the following April. Systrom and Krieger  left five months later.
 After the founder exodus, veteran Facebook executives Adam Mosseri and Will Cathcart took over Instagram and WhatsApp, respectively. Both were trusted Zuckerberg lieutenants and had previously overseen Facebook’s News Feed—setting the stage for the unification plan to come.
  For years, surveys have shown that most peo­ple  don’t know that Facebook owns Instagram and Whats­App. That often seemed to redound to the company’s favor. Folks who fled Facebook—­teenagers who deem it insufficiently cool or older users concerned about privacy—often ended up spending more time in something else owned by Facebook Inc.
 However, such ignorance became tougher to maintain in August 2019, when Instagram’s and WhatsApp’s startup screens began declaring that the apps were “From Facebook.” Tying beloved products to the embattled Facebook brand was a gutsy act of transparency, says Ivan Kayser, CEO of strategy and design consultancy Redscout, who calls Facebook’s willingness to do so “weirdly positive.”
 Confusion about the relationship between Facebook and the other services under its umbrella was “long-term, not a good thing for us,” says Instagram VP of product Vishal Shah. He points out that the company’s apps have always benefited from certain logical integrations and consolidations. For example, Instagram users have been able to cross-post photos to Facebook with one click. Businesses also have unified tools, such as a single dashboard for wrangling communications with customers on both Facebook and Instagram.
 But in many cases, Facebook had been willing to double up on its efforts, if not triple or quadruple. After Instagram Stories had become a blockbuster, in 2017, Facebook, Messenger, and Whats­App rolled out their own takes on the idea—pretty darn similar, but not identical. That’s an inefficient way to build new functionality. “Right now, every time we innovate on something in Facebook Stories, Instagram takes three months to implement that same feature inside Instagram Stories, and vice versa,” sighs Fidji Simo, who, as VP of the Facebook app, is responsible for the company’s flagship product.
  Contrast that with last spring’s rollout of Rooms, a Zoom-esque videoconferencing service that the company pushed out months ahead of schedule when the COVID-19 pandemic hit. Though officially part of Messenger, Rooms can also be launched from Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. Other recent launches, such as Facebook Pay and Facebook Shops, follow a similar one-service-everywhere strategy. (In August, the company folded all of its payments-related initiatives into a new group called Facebook Financial, led by former Messenger chief David Marcus—who was once president of PayPal.)
 New functionality, such as Accounts Center’s aggregation of Facebook and Instagram settings in one place, may sound mundane. But given an app like Instagram’s historic independence and cultural quirks, even seemingly straightforward tweaks face an array of challenges.
 On Facebook, for example, people are limited—at least in theory—to a single, real-identity account. On Instagram, having a pseudonym is fine, and some young people maintain a “fake Instagram,” or “finsta”: a second, secret account meant only for close friends. The last thing anyone would want is their finsta posts popping up on Facebook. “Users need to decide that [Facebook and Insta­gram accounts] can be interrelated,  and that that control should be on their side, from our perspective,” says George Lee, a Facebook director of product and former Instagram head of growth.
 The braiding together of sites has required adjustment on everyone’s part. Shah joined Instagram in 2015, when it was a more self-contained operation. Today, he says, “I tell people, ‘If you’re here because you miss the old days of how we used to build and experiment in a vacuum, maybe this isn’t the right place for you now.’ ” (That didn’t stop Instagram from recently  unveiling Reels, a TikTok doppelgänger that’s only available inside Insta­gram; Facebook says it has no current plans to spread it to other apps.)
 The trickiest part of Zuckerberg’s plan is the intensified emphasis on private messaging. Without setting a deadline, he said in his 2019 post that users of Messenger, Whats­App, and Instagram’s messaging feature—each historically a walled garden—would be able to communicate securely across all three services in a project referred to within the company as “interop.” The first tangible result is the new version of Messenger. It remains a standalone app—but the same features appear within Instagram to replace its existing messaging capabilities, which  have been  known as Instagram Direct since their 2013 debut. The update is going live in a few countries now, with a global rollout (including in the U.S.) planned for later in 2020.
 In part, turning Instagram’s messaging features into an in-app version of Messenger is about efficiency. Chief product officer Cox says that it will allow the company to “do the research, figure out the best product, build the best product, and then bring it.” Instagram users get to choose whether to switch or keep Instagram Direct in its existing form; there’s a lot to tempt them to make the move, including new selfie stickers, animated text effects, custom emoji reactions, Snapchat-like vanishing messages, and features for shared video watching—plus the ability to message friends on Messenger.
  Facebook says it has no plans to force every Instagram user to embrace the new experience. It seems unlikely, however, that the company will maintain two flavors of messaging in the app indefinitely. Those who cherish Instagram as a refuge from Facebook could see the Messenger-derived features as an encroachment rather than an upgrade. But Cox says that he’s been encouraged by tests: “It’s not interrupting any habit or pattern or something that people really love. We’re feeling pretty good about what we’ve seen in the folks who’ve used it so far.”
 The new Instagram messaging capabilities also include signs that Facebook thought about the privacy implications of knocking down walls between services. For instance, Instagram users can turn off the ability to receive messages from Messenger. Alternatively, they can automatically route particular types of incoming missives—say, ones from friends of Facebook friends—to Message Requests, where they’re quarantined rather than displayed. There’s even a feature that suggests accounts you might want to block based on who you’ve blocked on Facebook.
 “Everyone at Facebook now understands that if we don’t do [privacy and security] well, we won’t contribute to Facebook’s mission,” says product manager Mitu Singh, who now works on cross-app experiences. “It’s super ingrained in everyone’s heads. And I think in addition to that, they also realize that if we don’t do this well, they won’t get to ship the product experiences that they want to ship and that they’re excited about having people use.”
 For now, WhatsApp isn’t part of this mix, and given the app’s more stripped-down feel, any move to unify it with Messenger might leave users particularly skittish.  The  end-to-end encryption that Zuckerberg promised for Messenger and Instagram is also still to come. Messenger VP Chudnovsky won’t say when the interop project will be completed other than that it won’t be this year. “I assumed it was going to be very complicated,” he says. “I think it’s somewhat more complicated than we originally thought.”
 The challenges aren’t merely technical. Facebook’s history is pockmarked with examples of its playing fast and loose with its users’ data in service of its behemoth advertising business. The likelihood that Zuckerberg would face widespread cynicism about his vision of a more private future was so high that even he had to acknowledge it. “I understand that many people don’t think Facebook can or would even want to build this kind of privacy-focused platform—­because frankly we don’t currently have a strong reputation for building privacy protective services, and we’ve historically focused on tools for more open sharing,” he wrote.
 At least Zuckerberg could point to Whats­App as a model. Long after its disaffected founders quit, the service’s end-to-end encryption and general reputation for privacy remain intact. “They haven’t screwed it up, which is kind of neat,” says technologist and author Bruce Schneier, though he quickly adds that Facebook “is not going to do too much that doesn’t let them spy on you, because their business model depends on it.”
  If Facebook follows through with its promise to bring WhatsApp-level privacy to the rest of its services, a long-simmering tension over the tech industry’s relationship with the law is likely to boil over. Governments hate end-to-end encryption, because it makes messages impervious to standard monitoring techniques, even with the assistance of the tech company. Last October, U.S. Attorney General William Barr and officials from the U.K. and Australia signed  an open letter to Zuckerberg opposing his plans to spread end-to-end encryption throughout Facebook’s services, calling it a calamitous hindrance to investigations of “child sexual exploitation and abuse, terrorism, and foreign adversaries’ attempts to undermine democratic values and institutions.”
 Law enforcement officials often propose adding a “back door” to encryption schemes that would allow them access to data in appropriate circumstance

title: 看看Facebook是如何将Facebook、Instagram、Messenger和WhatsApp编织在一起的，尽管它受到了监管机构和监管机构越来越严格的审查
title_en: A look at how Facebook is weaving Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp together even as it comes under increasing scrutiny from watchdogs and regulators

note:
Facebook今天宣布，它将开始推出新功能，允许Instagram和Messenger用户通过应用程序进行交流，此外，还将把一系列受Messenger启发的功能引入Instagram收件箱。在Instagram上，用户将看到一个选项，可以更新到新的消息体验，提供更改聊天颜色、使用任何表情符号进行反应、一起观看视频、设置消息消失等功能。作为此次更新的一部分，他们还可以选择与使用Facebook的朋友聊天，应用程序会通知他们。
在Instagram收件箱中添加更多更“有趣”的内容将成为吸引用户同意升级的一种方式。这一决定反过来又将用户锁定在Facebook世界的更深一层。有了跨平台的消息互操作性，用户可能看不到尝试不同聊天应用的理由，因为一个消息应用可以通过世界上两个最大的社交网络联系到朋友和家人。
Facebook表示，即使Instagram用户没有Facebook账户，新的互操作性也会起作用，反之亦然。
随着时间的推移，Facebook计划将WhatsApp也纳入到体验中，以进一步巩固其市场力量。
尽管许多用户可能会选择更新以获得有趣的增强功能，但Facebook指出，事后他们可以选择不使用新的隐私控制来跨平台访问。
通过一套扩展的隐私工具，用户可以指定谁可以到达他们的主聊天列表，谁被发送到消息请求文件夹，以及谁根本无法联系到他们。如果Instagram用户不想在Facebook上听到任何人的消息，他们可以关闭这一功能。
这些控制也可以在Facebook昨天推出的新账户中心中进行管理。该工具允许用户管理越来越多的跨应用程序功能，如单点登录和Facebook Pay。
和以前一样，Instagram和Messenger应用程序的用户将能够根据需要拦截和报告可疑和有害的消息和电话。但屏蔽和报告将扩大到允许用户报告Instagram上的单个消息之外的完整对话。Messenger中的“安全通知”功能，帮助用户发现可疑活动并做出回应，也将出现在Instagram上-最初是针对未成年人的账户。
即使你同意跨平台访问，Facebook也澄清说它实际上并没有合并你的收件箱。
换句话说，你不会在Messenger中看到你所有的Instagram聊天，反之亦然。Instagram用户的消息和来自朋友和家人的电话将保留在Instagram应用程序中，但如果允许，这些消息现在可能包括Facebook用户发起的消息。
如果这些变化看起来有点令人困惑，那可能是有意为之。Facebook和Instagram用户不得不在错综复杂的隐私和安全设置中导航，随着Facebook网络提供的功能也在扩大，这些设置每年都会变得越来越复杂。尽管Facebook提供了一系列细微差别的控件，但许多用户不再费心去试图弄清楚它们，因为它们不断地变化、重新定位或变得更加复杂。
消费者可能只会将消息传递互操作性视为通过其他服务联系朋友的便捷方式。但对于行业观察家来说，这是Facebook似乎正在利用其市场主导地位可能扼杀新竞争对手的又一个例子。对于一家已经受到多项反垄断调查的公司来说，此举似乎是对政府监管机构嗤之以鼻。
从基础设施的角度来看，让Facebook的聊天平台互操作的项目是一项重大的技术承诺。去年，Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)详细介绍了公司在消息互操作性方面的计划，这是他更私密的社交网络体验的更大愿景的一部分。
在互操作性之外的更大更新方面，Instagram用户还可以一起观看视频，包括Facebook Watch和Soon Reels的视频。
他们还可以像Snapchat一样，通过“消失模式”选项让他们的消息消失。其他新功能包括类似回旋镖的“自拍贴纸”，可以个性化聊天的颜色，使用自定义的表情符号反应，转发最多五个朋友或群的消息，为了清晰起见直接回复群聊中的特定消息，以及使用动画效果为消息添加视觉效果。
Facebook表示，这些功能将开始向公众推出，最初在世界上的几个国家推出，然后在全球范围内扩展。
note_en:
Facebook    announced today it will begin rolling out new functionality that will allow Instagram and Messenger users to communicate across apps, in addition to bringing a host of  Messenger  -inspired features to the  Instagram   inbox. On Instagram, users will be presented with an option to update to a new messaging experience that offers the ability to change your chat color, react with any emoji, watch videos together, set messages to disappear and more. As a part of this update, they’ll also have the option to chat with friends who use Facebook, the app will inform them.
  The broad set of more “fun” additions to the Instagram inbox will serve as a way to entice users to agree to the upgrade. This decision, in turn, locks users further inside the Facebook universe. With cross-platform messaging interoperability, users may see fewer reasons to try a different chat app as one messaging app can reach friends and family across two of the world’s largest social networks.
  Facebook says the new interoperability will also work even if the Instagram users don’t have a Facebook account, and vice versa.
 In time, Facebook plans to fold WhatsApp into the experience, too, in a further consolidation of its market power.
  Though many users may choose to update for the fun enhancements, Facebook notes they can then opt out of being reachable across platforms using new privacy controls, after the fact.
 Through an expanded set of privacy tools, users can specify who can reach their main Chats list, who is sent to the Message Request folder and who can’t reach them at all. If an Instagram user doesn’t want to hear from anyone on Facebook, they can turn this feature off.
  These controls can also be managed in the new Accounts Center, which Facebook  launched yesterday. The tool allows users to manage a growing set of cross-app features, like Single Sign On and Facebook Pay.
 As before, users on both Instagram and Messenger apps will be able to block and report suspicious and unwanted messages and calls on an as-needed basis. But blocking and reporting will be  expanded to allow users to report full conversations in addition to single messages on Instagram.  The “Safety Notices” feature in Messenger, which helps users spot and respond to suspicious activity, will also come to Instagram — initially to minors’ accounts.
  Even if you agree to being reachable across platforms, Facebook clarifies that it’s not actually merging your inboxes.
 In other words, you won’t see all your Instagram chats in Messenger or vice versa. Instagram users’ messages and calls from friends and family will remain in the Instagram app, but these may now include messages initiated by a Facebook user, if permitted.
 If these changes seem a bit confusing, that could be by design. Facebook and Instagram users have to navigate a labyrinth of privacy and security settings that  grow more complicated every year as the functionality offered by Facebook’s networks also expands. Though Facebook offers a range of nuanced controls, many users no longer bother to try to figure them out, as they’re constantly changing, relocated or made more complex.
 Consumers may only view the messaging interoperability as a handy way to reach their friends on other services. But for industry observers, it’s another example of how Facebook appears to be leveraging its market dominance to possibly stifle new competition. For a company already under multiple  antitrust investigations, it’s a move that seems to thumb its nose at government regulators.
  The project to make Facebook’s chat platforms interoperate has been a significant technical undertaking from an infrastructure perspective. Last year, Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg  detailed the company’s plans for messaging interoperability as part of his larger vision for a more private social networking experience.
  In terms of the larger update beyond interoperability, Instagram users will also be able to watch videos together, including those from Facebook Watch and soon Reels.
  They’ll also be able to make their messages disappear, like Snapchat, with a “Vanish Mode” option. Other new features include Boomerang-like “Selfie Stickers,” the ability to personalize the chat’s colors, use custom emoji reactions, forward messages with up to five friends or groups, reply directly to a specific message in a group chat for clarity’s sake and add visual flair to messages with animated effects.
  Facebook says the features will begin rolling out to the general public, initially with a handful of countries around the world before expanding globally.
title: Facebook推出Messenger和Instagram之间的跨应用通信，即使Instagram用户没有Facebook账户也可以使用，反之亦然
title_en: Facebook unveils cross-app communication between Messenger and Instagram, which works even if an Instagram user doesn't have a Facebook account and vice versa

当然，我花了6天时间积累了3626笔交易，总计101,528美元，但就像任何一夜成名一样，它也是多年酝酿而成的。对我来说，大约7年。
2013年，IOS的越狱之日全面生效。受此启发，我创作了一套图标，并在Cydia商店以0.99美元的价格出售了一套名为“灰色高清”(Greyish HD&39；)的图标。这是我在互联网上赚的头几美元。我想我总共赚了17美元，太神奇了。
很明显，这不是17美元的问题，而是我如何赚到的。我花了一些时间创建一些我认为很酷的东西，然后点击发布。几天后，我以前从未见过的陌生人决定把他们辛苦赚来的钱花在我的图标套装上，通常是在我做完全不同的东西的时候。在没有意识到的情况下，我刚刚发现了数字内容的杠杆作用：花足够的力气创造一次东西，然后用最少的努力重复销售它。
快进7年后，减去6天。我看到一些人在发现iOS14现在允许你使用Siri快捷键应用程序在你的主屏幕上添加定制图标后，分享了他们iPhone的屏幕截图。这是你第一次可以真正定制iOS，而且它正在流行起来。
这是人们多年来在越狱iOS和Android设备上一直在做的事情，除了这一次，你可以在iOS上原生地做到这一点。
当我注意到炒作的时候，我就按照我自己的风格组装了一些图标，下载了一些小工具，并尝试了所有的东西。我觉得它看起来很酷，所以我在推特上分享了一张截图。立刻，人们开始询问截图中的图标。所以我迅速将它们打包，上传到GumRoad，并使用Super将它们嵌入到一个概念站点上。所有这一切花了大约两个小时。
第二天，这条推文获得了数百次转发，数千个赞，超过10万次印象。后天，差不多有一百万。接下来我知道的是，它到处都是。我的图标被发布在著名的科技网站上，比如cult of Mac，iMore，AllThingsTech和Gridfiti。我想这个时候我的销售额在6000美元左右。
然后，MKBHD发生了。他分享了一段关于这一切的视频-使用我的图标进行设置，并在描述中链接了这些图标。接下来我知道的是，我赚了28美元，感觉就像是每28秒赚一次。我的手机变成了终极的多巴胺分配器(如果它还不是的话)。我不得不禁用通知功能。
第二天，销售额从6K美元跃升至4万美元左右，在撰写本文期间，来自4188名客户的销售额为116,147美元。
正确的内容，在正确的时间发布，可以产生难以想象的结果。虽然可能有很多其他的变数导致了这一结果，但我认为有几个关键的洞察力增加了我的胜算。
不断地把东西放到这个世界上会创造出工作的证据。你现在的位置和你想要的位置之间的区别可能只是分享你建造的东西，甚至是你知道的东西。
一旦把一些东西推到这个世界上，我们只能控制这么多，但出版往往会增加你找到坚定不移的东西的机会。他们说命运眷顾胆大包天的人。在互联网时代，大胆的人是那些不怕将自己的作品公之于众的人。互联网是永无止境的内容流，让人讨厌或过度分享的想法只是你发明的一个阻止你分享的想法。
我本可以轻而易举地忽略对图标的请求，或者将人们引导到已经存在的图标集，但这将是一个错失的机会。因为我在过去已经做过很多次类似的图标设置了，所以我已经准备好了。如果你打算乘势而上，快速行动是至关重要的。
我在我的主屏幕上贴出所有炒作的细节后第二天分享了这条推文，它制造了更多的炒作。它又带来了54万人的印象，并为已经在增长的大火火上浇油。分享有效的东西，分享无效的东西。透明就是可见性。
很多这样的事情都发生在一个周一的早上。如果我从事的是一份更传统的工作，或者有我之前安排的时间安排，我就不会像以前那样迅速地将我的想法付诸行动。我知道，并不是每个人都享有弹性时间表的特权，但如果他们足够迫切地想要的话，几乎每个人都可以开始朝着这个目标前进。明确时间表的自由让我一有灵感就能采取行动，这对于最大限度地扩大曝光率是极其重要的。灵感是生产力的倍增器，但它很容易腐烂--所以要快点行动。
如果我问任何人这些设备的价格是多少，大多数人会说是2美元，或者可能是5美元。我可以肯定的一点是，最有可能购买这些设备的人与过去越狱的iOS设备不是同一个人。这些都是第一次使用iOS定制器，所以不知道iOS图标集的价格应该是多少。
如果我这样做完全是为了赚钱，我确信它不会像它那样工作得那么好。我看到到处都是模仿者。他们看到了成功，并试图准确复制它是如何发生的，但这是行不通的。至少不是那么好。写这篇文章的原因不是教你如何坐在座位边上，抓住机会利用互联网的下一个大趋势，而是要推动你继续做你喜欢的事情，与世界分享它们，让互联网做剩下的事情。(编者注：这篇文章的目的不是教你如何坐在椅子边上，抓住机会抓住下一个互联网大趋势，而是鼓励你继续做你喜欢的事情，与世界分享它们，让互联网来做剩下的事情)。
那么这一切的结果是什么呢？我怀疑这就是你们大多数人在这里阅读这篇文章的原因，所以下面是上周以来的数字回顾，从我写这篇文章的时候开始：
就像7年前创建图标套装让我为上周做好准备一样，整个实验可能也让我为几年后的事情做好了准备。
最好的部分是它为我赢得了自由，让我继续建造能创造更多自由的东西。把时间花在能为你赢得时间的事情上总是很好的利用时间。当人们买进你转售的任何东西时，他们不仅给了你钱，而且还为你未来的自由做出了贡献。
市场将在很大程度上决定分享后会发生什么，所以不断地通过建立、发布然后重复来增加你的机会。很多事情一次试一次，想清楚你想做什么，再试一次。找出你大脑中储存的对他人有价值的东西，然后与世界分享。你可能会对它的结果感到惊讶。
note_en:
Sure, it took me 6 days to amass 3,626 sales for a total of $101,528, but like any overnight success, it was years in the making. In my case, about 7 years.
 In 2013, the jailbreaking days of iOS were in full effect. Inspired, I created and sold an icon set titled 'Greyish HD' on the Cydia store for $0.99. It was the first few dollars I ever made on the internet. I think I made a total of $17, and it was magical.
 It wasn't about the $17, obviously; it was how I earned it. I spent time creating something that I thought was cool, then hit publish. After a few days, random strangers I've never met before decided to spend their hard-earned cents on my icon set, oftentimes while I was working on something else entirely. Without realizing it, I just discovered digital content leverage:  create something once with sufficient effort, then sell it repeatedly with minimal effort.
 Fast forward 7 years later, minus 6 days. I saw some people sharing screenshots of their iPhones after discovering that iOS 14 now allows you to add custom icons to your home screen using the Siri Shortcuts app. This was the first time you can really customize iOS, and it was catching on.
 This is something people have been doing for years on jailbroken iOS and Android devices, except this time, you can do it natively on iOS.
 As soon as I noticed the hype, I put together some  icons in my own style, downloaded some widgets, and tried it all out. I thought it looked cool, so I shared a screenshot of it on Twitter. Right away, people started asking about the icons in the screenshot. So I quickly packaged them, uploaded them to  Gumroad, and embedded them on a  Notion site using  Super. All of this took about two hours.
 The next day, the tweet had hundreds of retweets, thousands of likes, and over 100k impressions. The day after that, almost a million. The next thing I knew, it was everywhere. My icons got published on notable tech sites like  Cult of Mac,  iMore,  AllThingsTech, and  Gridfiti. I think at this time I was around the $6k mark in sales.
 Then, MKBHD happened. He shared a  video about all of this—using my icons for his setup, and linked them in the description. The next thing I knew, I was making $28 what felt like every 28 seconds. My phone turned into the ultimate dopamine dispenser (if it wasn't already). I had to disable notifications.
 The day after, sales jumped from $6k to about $40k, and during the time of this writing, sales are at $116,147 from 4,188 customers.
  The right content, posted at the right time, can create unimaginable results. Although there's likely plenty of other variables that went into making this work, there are a few key insights that I think increased my odds.
  Continually putting things out into the world creates proof of work. The difference between where you are and where you want to be could be as little as sharing what you build, or even what you know.
 There's only so much we can control once pushing something out into the world, but publishing often will increase your odds at finding something that sticks. They say that fortune favors the bold. In the internet age, the bold are those that aren't afraid to publish their work for the world to see. The internet is a never-ending stream of content, the idea of being annoying or over-sharing is only an idea that you invent to stop you from sharing.
  I could have easily ignored the requests for the icons, or directed people to icon sets that already existed, but that would be a missed opportunity. Since I've done similar icon sets so many times already in the past, I was ready for it. Acting quickly is crucial if you plan on riding the wave.
  I shared  this tweet the day after posting my home screen with details of all the hype, and it created even more hype. It brought another 540,000 impressions and added fuel to an already growing fire.  Share what works, and share what doesn't. Transparency is visibility.
  A lot of this was happening on a Monday morning. If I were working a more traditional job, or had time commitments that I had previously scheduled, I wouldn't have been able to act on my ideas as quickly as I did. I get that not everyone has the privilege of a flexible schedule, but almost anyone can start to move towards it if they want it badly enough. Freedom of a clear schedule allowed me to take action as soon as the inspiration hit, which was incredibly important to maximize exposure. Inspiration is a productivity-multiplier, but it's perishable—so act quickly.
  If I would have asked anyone what to price these at, most would have said $2, or maybe $5. One thing I knew for sure was that the people most likely to buy these were not the same people who were jailbreaking their iOS devices in the past. These are first time iOS customizers, so there's no notion of what an iOS icon set should be priced at.
  If I would have done this exclusively for the goal of making money, I'm convinced it wouldn't have worked nearly as well as it did. I see copycats showing up everywhere. They see success, and try to replicate exactly how it happened, but it won't work. At least not nearly as well. The reasons for this post is not to teach you how to sit on the edge of your seat, foaming at the mouth at the chance to exploit the next big internet trend, but it's to push you to keep working at the things you enjoy, share them with the world, and letting the internet do the rest.
  So what came of all this? I suspect this is why most of you are here reading this, so here's a recap of everything that came from last week in numbers, from the time of this writing:
      In the same way that creating icon sets 7 years ago prepared me for last week, this whole experiment has likely prepared me for what's to come years from now.
 The best part about this is the freedom its bought me, to keep building things that'll create even more freedom.  Spending time on things that will buy you time is always a good use of it. When people buy into what ever it is you're selling, they're not only giving you money, but they're contributing to your future freedom.
 The market will decide a huge part of what comes after sharing something, so continually increase your odds by building, publishing, then repeating. Try many things once to figure out what you want to do twice. Figure out what you've got stored in your brain that can be of value to others, then share it with the world. You might be surprised of what comes of it.
title: 六天六位数

有消息称，自8月初以来，SpaceX的Starlink卫星一直在向华盛顿州军方的应急管理部门提供互联网服务，帮助居民从最近的野火中恢复过来。
CNBC指出，为华盛顿紧急响应人员提供服务是卫星宽带服务的第一个公开应用。
Starlink在7月份发布在Reddit上的FAQ中透露，SpaceX目前正在与美国北部和加拿大下部一些地区的居民进行私人Starlink测试版试验，包括华盛顿州的偏远社区。
华盛顿应急部门一直在使用7个Starlink用户终端，SpaceX公司的埃隆·马斯克此前曾将其描述为类似于棍子上的UFO，其直径为48厘米(19英寸)，朝向天空。
马斯克此前曾形容终端用户终端安装起来就像指着天空一样容易，只需插上电源即可。
华盛顿州军方IT部门的紧急电信负责人理查德·霍尔(Richard Hall)似乎证实了马斯克的说法。
霍尔在接受CNBC采访时表示：我从来没有安装过像[星联]一样快速可靠的战术卫星设备。
霍尔还表示，Starlink优于他的部门之前使用过的其他卫星宽带服务。StarLink卫星围绕地球运行的高度约为500公里(311英里)，比传统的传统卫星宽带服务更接近地球。
根据霍尔的说法，Starlink提供的带宽是其他服务的两倍，他还说他看到延迟减少了150%以上。他说，我看到的延迟一直低于30毫秒。
对于Starlink来说，这是一个相当好的第三方参考，该公司一直面临联邦通信委员会(Federal Communications Commission)的质疑，怀疑它能否提供低于50ms的往返延迟，FCC在申请发射3万颗卫星时声称这一延迟低于50ms。事实上，霍尔的体验更接近马斯克之前声称的20毫秒。
SpaceX需要向FCC证明，它可以提供低延迟服务，以优化其获得FCC高达160亿美元的农村数字机会基金(RDOF)部分资金的机会，以将宽带带到目前网速低于25 Mbps的600万家庭和企业。
但是，SpaceX的Starlink卫星星座由不到800颗Starlink卫星组成，与FCC批准发射的12000颗卫星相比，这只是一小部分。
霍尔说，与其他卫星地面单元相比，设置和连接Starlink终端需要5到10分钟。
马斯克回应了西澳应急管理部门披露其使用Starlink卫星的推文，称SpaceX正在优先考虑紧急响应人员和根本没有互联网连接的地点。
根据霍尔的说法，华盛顿的自然资源部和国土安全部的联邦紧急事务管理署也对试验Starlink卫星宽带服务感兴趣。
很高兴得到@SpaceX的Starlink互联网的支持，紧急救援人员希望帮助居民重建本月早些时候被野火吞没的华盛顿州马尔登镇。#waWildfire pic.twitter.com/xUSQOjcT4T
-西澳应急管理😷(@waemd)2020年9月28日。
SpaceX在最近一次帮助其RDOF应用程序的演示中展示了互联网性能测试，下载速度在102 Mbps到103 Mbps之间，上传速度在40.5 Mbps到42 Mbps之间，延迟在18毫秒到19毫秒之间。
SpaceX的Starlink雄心现在还为时尚早。该公司已向联邦通信委员会申请在美国部署500万台最终用户终端，但目前仅按计划每月生产数千台消费者用户终端。
SpaceX定于美国东部时间10月2日(星期五)晚上9点43分发射另一批60颗卫星。这次发射标志着它的第13次Starlink任务，应该会将其卫星总数推高到800多颗，马斯克曾表示，这是适度覆盖北美所必需的。
目标是10月1日(星期四)上午9：17。美国东部夏令时(EDT)，用于发射Starlink。由于射程上的冲突，现在的目标是在10月2日星期五发射GPS III-4；15分钟的窗口在晚上9点43分开始。美国东部夏令时pic.twitter.com/VVhhatjBbh。
-SpaceX(@SpaceX)2020年9月30日
note_en:
It's emerged that SpaceX's Starlink satellites have been delivering internet services since early August to the Washington state military's emergency management unit helping residents recover from recent wildfires.
 As noted by CNBC, providing services to Washington emergency responders is the first publicly known application of the satellite broadband service.
    SpaceX is currently conducting private Starlink beta trials with residents in some parts of northern US and lower Canada, including remote communities in Washington state, Starlink  revealed in an FAQ posted on Reddit in July.
  The Washington emergency division has been using seven Starlink user terminals, which SpaceX Elon Musk has previously described as like a "UFO on a stick", with a skyward-facing disk that measures 48cm, or 19 inches, in diameter.
 Musk has previously described the end-user terminals as being as easy to set up as " point at sky and just plug in".
 Richard Hall, the emergency telecommunications leader of the Washington State Military Department's IT division, appears to confirm Musk's claim.
   "I have never set up any tactical satellite equipment that has been as quick to set up and anywhere near as reliable [as Starlink]," Hall told CNBC.
 Hall also suggested Starlink was superior to other satellite broadband services his unit has used previously. Starlink satellites orbit Earth at an altitude of about 500km, or 311 miles, far closer to Earth than traditional conventional satellite broadband services.
 According to Hall, Starlink offers double the bandwidth of other services and said he'd seen more than 150% decreases in latency. "I've seen lower than 30 millisecond latency consistently," he said.
 That's a pretty good third-party reference for Starlink, which has faced doubts from the Federal Communications Commission as to whether it can deliver round-trip latencies below the 50ms that it has  claimed in an FCC application to launch 30,000 satellites. In fact, Hall's experience is closer to  the 20ms Musk has previously claimed.
 SpaceX needs to prove to the FCC it can deliver a low-latency service to optimize its chances of securing part of the FCC's up-to $16bn  Rural Digital Opportunity Fund (RDOF) to bring broadband to six million homes and businesses with current speeds below 25Mbps.
  But SpaceX's Starlink satellite constellation of fewer than 800 Starlink satellites is just a fraction of the 12,000 satellites it has been approved by the FCC to launch.
 Hall said it took between five and 10 minutes to set up and connect a Starlink terminal compared with other satellite ground units.
 Musk responded to WA Emergency Management's tweet disclosing its use of Starlink satellites,  saying that SpaceX was "prioritizing emergency responders and locations with no internet connectivity at all".
 According to Hall, Washington's Department of Natural Resources and the Department of Homeland Security's Federal Emergency Management Agency are also interested in trialing Starlink satellite broadband services.
 Happy to have the support of  @SpaceX’s Starlink internet as emergency responders look to help residents rebuild the town of Malden, WA that was overcome by wildfires earlier this month.  #wawildfire  pic.twitter.com/xUSQOjcT4T
— WA Emergency Management 😷 (@waEMD)  September 28, 2020
 In a  recent presentation in aid of its RDOF application, SpaceX showed internet performance tests with download speeds of between 102Mbps to 103Mbps, upload speeds of 40.5Mbps to not quite 42Mbps, and a latency of 18 milliseconds to 19 milliseconds.
 It's still early days for SpaceX's Starlink ambitions. The company has applied to the FCC to deploy five million end-user terminals in the US, but it is currently only "on track to produce thousands of consumer user terminals per month".
 SpaceX is scheduled to launch another batch of 60 satellites on Friday, October 2 at 9:43pm EDT. This launch marks its 13th Starlink mission and should nudge its total satellite count to over 800, which Musk has said is required for moderate coverage of North America.
 Targeting Thursday, October 1 at 9:17 a.m. EDT for launch of Starlink. Due to a conflict on the Range, now targeting launch of GPS III-4 on Friday, October 2; 15-min window opens at 9:43 p.m. EDT  pic.twitter.com/VVhhatjBbh
— SpaceX (@SpaceX)  September 30, 2020
title: SpaceX的Starlink在行动：互联网卫星让紧急救援人员保持在线
title_en: SpaceX's Starlink in action: Internet satellites keep emergency workers online

从纸面上看，备忘录银行的活期账户看起来很像一个软件即服务产品。有三种不同的计划。每月49欧元，你可以获得一个用户账户，每个额外的账户每月收费10欧元。你每月有20笔交易进进出出，每笔额外的交易费用是0.40欧元。
每月149欧元，您可以创建任意数量的用户帐户，每月可获得200笔交易。再一次，额外的交易每笔交易收费0.40欧元。
如果你处理大量交易，每月399欧元就可以获得无限交易。中端计划还允许您访问授权透支。
有趣的是，排名前两位的公司将从他们的存款中赚取利息-前两个计划分别为0.15%至10万欧元和0.30%至20万欧元。备忘录银行暂时不提支票或支付卡。
这家初创公司还表示，它的网络平台应该比一般的银行网站运行得更好。搜索功能按预期工作，您可以发布分组转账来支付员工工资，您还可以设置大额交易的审批工作流。
更重要的是，备忘录银行开放业务发放贷款。公司可以申请获得2万至20万欧元的贷款，并在1至7年内偿还。有了这款产品，这家初创公司正在与10月份等在线贷款平台竞争。
note_en:
Earlier this year,  Memo Bank   obtained licenses from the French regulator (ACPR) and the European Central Bank to become a credit institution. It can provide all the services you’d expect from a business bank, from current accounts to credit lines.
 On paper, Memo Bank’s current accounts look a lot like a software-as-a-service product. There are three different plans. For €49 per month, you get one user account and each additional account costs €10 per month. You get 20 transactions in and out per month, each additional transaction costs €0.40 per transaction.
  For €149 per month, you can create as many user accounts as you want and you get 200 transactions per month. Once again, additional transactions cost €0.40 per transaction.
 And if you handle a lot of transactions, you get unlimited transactions for €399 per month. The mid-tier plan also lets you access an authorized overdraft.
 Interestingly, companies on the top two tiers will earn interests on their deposits — 0.15% up to €100,000 and 0.30% up to €200,000 for the top two plans respectively. Memo Bank isn’t mentioning checks or payment cards for now.
  The startup is also saying that its web platform should work better than your average banking site. The search feature works as expected, you can issue grouped transfers to pay your employees and you can set up an approval workflow for big transactions.
 More importantly, Memo Bank is open for business to issue loans. Companies can apply to get a €20,000 to €200,000 loan and pay back over 1 to 7 years. With this product, the startup is competing with online lending platforms, such as  October.
title: 备忘录银行详细说明其为其商业银行账户提供的服务
title_en: Memo Bank details its offering for its business bank accounts

转向多策略投资代表着公司可支配的融资工具的多样化，并为这家私人股本公司提供了在资本泛滥的世界中竞争所需的工具，以及公司获得公开市场融资的新方式。
随着越来越多的科技公司避开后期融资，通过特殊目的收购公司(SPACS)等方式在公开市场上寻找现金，公私合营、只做多头的投资结构正在发生，这可能并不是巧合。
根据该公司的一份声明，新战略的部署生命周期为25年，可以跨结构、地理位置和行业进行投资。这项协议使这两个金融实体成为一对真正跨越时间的情侣。
除了新的战略，银湖的合作伙伴关系在阿布扎比支持的主权财富基金中又有了一个新的少数股东。穆巴达拉收购了银湖合伙人出售给纽伯格·伯曼(Neuberger Berman)子公司戴尔资本合伙公司(DYAL Capital Partners)10%的公司股份的一半，从而获得了该公司的少数股权。
DYAL Capital Partners董事总经理兼负责人迈克尔·里斯(Michael Rees)在一份声明中表示：“Silver Lake是DYAL表现最好的公司，它进行了创新、发展和扩张，谨慎地将其管理的资产从我们第一次收购股份时的230亿美元增加到今天的600多亿美元，”DYAL Capital Partners董事总经理兼负责人迈克尔·里斯(Michael Rees)在一份声明中表示。与穆巴达拉的这笔交易及其对银湖公司新的长期资本工具的承诺是对银湖公司差异化的全球能力的强烈认可，并强调了我们相信有能力通过持有世界领先的私人投资公司的股份来产生令人信服的回报。“。
这已经不是两家公司第一次联手了。穆巴达拉与银湖一起，是人才经纪和娱乐巨头奋进(Endeavor)、自动驾驶汽车技术开发商Waymo以及印度Jio平台的联合投资者。
该公司联席首席执行官埃贡·德班(Egon德班)和格雷格·蒙德雷(Greg Mondre)在一份联合声明中说，新交易将使公司能够利用一系列投资机会，包括现有基金授权之外的机会。
穆巴达拉董事总经理兼首席执行官哈尔顿·阿尔·穆巴拉克(Khaldoon Al Mubarak)在一份声明中表示：“作为一家长期看到投资科技行业潜力的机构，我们很高兴能与世界上最受尊敬的科技投资者之一银湖(Silver Lake)合作，利用行业内外的重大机遇。科技是全球经济的基石，也是所有其他正在显著数字化的行业的基础。我们的目标是处于有利地位，能够利用这种加速的数字转型及其潜力，我们相信银湖是正确的合作伙伴，对我们来说，这是一个最优的结构。”在一份声明中，穆巴达拉的董事总经理兼首席执行官哈尔顿·阿尔·穆巴拉克(Khaldoon Al Mubarak)表示：“作为一家机构，我们很高兴能与世界上最受尊敬的科技投资者之一银湖合作，利用行业内外的重大机遇。”
穆巴达拉的技术组合投资始于2007年，当时他投资了芯片制造商AMD，然后创建了半导体制造公司GlobalFoundries。该公司表示，它还支持医疗科技公司PCI Pharma Services，以及阿布扎比和硅谷的一些拼车和电子商务公司。
与银湖的交易也可以被视为对软银的一记耳光-穆巴达拉的长期合作伙伴，穆巴达拉是这家日本投资公司1000亿美元远景基金(Vision Fund)的投资者，也是去年2月推出的4亿美元专注于欧洲的投资工具。
note_en:
The move into multi-strategy investing represents the diversification of financing vehicles that companies have at their disposal and gives the private equity firm the tools it needs to compete in a world awash with capital and new ways for companies to access public market financing.
 It’s probably not a coincidence that the public-private, long-only, investment structure is happening as more tech companies are eschewing later stage financing to find cash on public markets through things like special purpose acquisition companies (SPACS).
   According to a statement from the firm, the new strategy has a 25-year deployment life cycle and can be invested across structures, geographies and industries. The agreement makes the two financial entities a couple  that will really span time together.
 In addition to the new strategy,  Silver Lake’s   partnership has a new minority shareholder in the Abu Dhabi-backed sovereign wealth fund. Mubadala took a minority stake in the firm by buying up half of the 10% chunk of the firm that Silver Lake’s partners sold to Dyal Capital Partners, a subsidiary of Neuberger Berman.
 “Silver Lake is a top performer for Dyal, having innovated, evolved and expanded to prudently grow its assets under management from $23 billion when we first acquired our stake to more than $60 billion today,” said Michael Rees, Managing Director and Head of Dyal Capital Partners, in a statement. “This transaction with Mubadala and their commitment to Silver Lake’s new long-term capital vehicle is a strong endorsement of Silver Lake’s differentiated, global capabilities and underscores our conviction in the ability to generate compelling returns by owning stakes in the world’s leading private investment firms.”
 It’s not the first time that the two firms have hooked up. Mubadala is a co-investor alongside Silver Lake in the talent agency and entertainment giant, Endeavor; the autonomous vehicle technology developer,  Waymo;   and the India-based Jio Platforms.
 The firm’s co-chief executives Egon Durban and Greg Mondre said in a joint statement that the new deal would allow the firm to capitalize on a wide range of investment opportunities, including ones outside of the mandates of existing funds.
 “As an institution that has long seen the potential of investing in the technology sector, we are excited to partner with Silver Lake, one of the world’s most respected technology investors, to capitalize on major opportunities within and beyond the industry,” said Khaldoon Al Mubarak, Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Mubadala, in a statement.  “Technology is the bedrock of the global economy, and fundamental to all other sectors that are being significantly digitalized.  Our goal is to be well positioned to take advantage of this accelerated digital transformation and its potential, and we believe Silver Lake is the right partner and that this is an optimal structure for us.”
 Mubadala’s tech portfolio investments kicked off in 2007 with an investment in the chip manufacturer  AMD   and then through the creation of the semiconductor manufacturing company GlobalFoundries. It’s also backed the medtech company PCI Pharma Services, and a number of ridesharing and e-commerce companies in Abu Dhabi and Silicon Valley, the company said.
 The deal with Silver Lake could also be seen as a slap in the face for Softbank — a long time partner for Mubadala, which was an investor in the Japanese investment firm’s $100 billion Vision Fund and a $400 million European-focused investment vehicle which launched in February of last year.

note: 在中断了近两年之后，被广泛称为印度消费者互联网故事中的大牛的前老虎环球基金经理李·菲克塞尔(Lee Fixel)重新开始投资印度的初创企业。Fixel今年早些时候在美国推出了自己的12亿美元增资基金，他领导了一轮3500万美元的InShort融资，这是一个聚合新闻和信息的平台。2015年，当他在老虎全球(Tiger Global)工作时，他第一次投资了InShort，当时他为这家初创公司开出了400万美元的支票。他在印度的第一笔新押注，正值总部位于纽约的老虎环球(Tiger Global)在斯科特·施莱弗(Scott Shleifer)的领导下积极在印度投资之际。施莱弗去年在Fixel离职后接管了该公司的私人股本投资组合。该基金最近的大笔投资包括Zomato和Byju；s。阅读：老虎基金的斯科特·施莱弗如何赶上李·菲克塞尔投资印度初创企业的步伐回应ET的质疑，InShort联合创始人兼首席执行官艾扎尔·伊夸巴尔表示，该公司已从Addition、SIG Global和唐林风险投资伙伴公司(Tanglin Venture Partners)那里筹集了3500万美元。InShort将利用这笔资金推动其最近推出的应用程序Public，这是一个基于位置的社交网络。该公司表示，该平台用于当地商业、就业、分类广告等方面的本地更新和活动。我们在InShort是盈利的，并为我们的新应用程序Public筹集了这笔资金。Iqubal表示，Public拥有超过5000万活跃用户，每月在该应用上创建超过100万个视频，其目标是连接巴拉特当地社区，成为满足他们所有当地需求的一站式解决方案。阅读：Lee Fixel将离开Tiger Global，将在印度和其他地方独自狩猎一位消息人士早些时候告诉ET，InShort的收入为1300万美元，扣除利息、税项、折旧和摊销前的收益为500万美元。Fixel&39；在印度最大的胜利来自2018年Flipkart以160亿美元出售给沃尔玛。然而，在收购之后，Tiger开始不再只专注于消费者互联网公司，也开始押注于企业和SaaS公司。此外，Fixel还投资了跨境支付处理器DLocal、心理健康初创公司Lyra Health、开发者优先的安全公司Snyk等。另请阅读：是什么导致老虎环球积极重返印度
note_en: After a hiatus of almost two years,  Lee Fixel, the media shy former  Tiger Global fund manager, known widely as the big bull of  India's consumer Internet story, is back to investing in local  startups.  Fixel, who launched his own $1.2 billion fund  Addition in the US earlier this year, has led a $35 million round in  Inshorts, a platform which aggregates news and information. He had first backed Inshorts while at Tiger Global in 2015, having cut a $4 million cheque for the startup. His first new bet in India comes at a time when New York-based Tiger Global has been actively investing in the country under Scott Shleifer, who  took over the private equity portfolio of the firm last year after Fixel's departure. The fund's recent big investments include Zomato and Byju's.   Read:  How Tiger's Scott Shleifer is matching Lee Fixel’s pace of investments in Indian startups   Responding to ET's query, Inshorts cofounder and CEO, Azhar Iqubal, said the company had raised $35 million from Addition, SIG Global and Tanglin Venture Partners. Inshorts will use the funds to push its recently launched app Public, a location-based social network. The platform is used for local updates and happenings to local commerce, jobs, classifieds and more, the company said.   "We are profitable at Inshorts and raised this capital for our new app Public. With more than 50 million active users and over 1 million videos being created every month on the app, Public aims to connect the local communities of Bharat and become the one-stop solution for all their local needs," Iqubal said.   Read:  Lee Fixel to leave Tiger Global, set to hunt alone in India and beyond  A source had earlier told ET that Inshorts was clocking $13 million in revenue with $5 million of earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation.  Fixel's  biggest win in India came from Flipkart’s  $16 billion sale to Walmart in 2018. After the acquisition though, Tiger started moving away from only focussing on consumer internet firms and began taking wagers on enterprise and SaaS companies, too.  From Addition, Fixel has invested in DLocal, a cross-border payment processor, mental health startup Lyra Health, developer-first security company Snyk, among others.   Also Read:  What has led to Tiger Global's aggressive comeback to India
